ocr.text: The data presented in the table offers a comprehensive overview of the physical capability of children in performing sit-ups
without assistance, segmented by age groups. Specifically, the columns labeled “≤ 15.0 years of age” and “> 15.0 years of age”
provide insight into the percentage of children capable of executing this fundamental exercise within these distinct age brackets.
Notably, the data indicates that 68.6% of children aged 15.0 years or younger can perform sit-ups independently, compared to
71.1% in those older than 15.0 years. Additionally, the table includes genetic information, such as the SNP rs1790858, with
corresponding metrics like minor allele frequency (MAF) and odds ratios, which may suggest a correlation between genetic
factors and physical fitness capabilities.
Survey item  PatientsN (%)**
≤ 15.0 years of age  > 15.0 years of age
Right now, can your child do a sit-up without help?  142 (68.6)  32 (71.1)
Right now, can your child climb the stairs without help or using support?  168 (81.2)  36 (80.0)
Right now, can your child stand from a seated position without assistance or support?  179 (86.5)  35 (77.8)
Right now, does your child have a swallowing problem?  12 (5.8)  4 (8.9)
RA case genotypes  RA-free control genotypes  Association results
SNP  11  12  22  MAF  P(HWE)  11  12  22  MAF  P(HWE)  Pa  Odds ratio for minor allele (95% CI)a
Consumption Time  Total  Single dose in a day  Double doses in a day
Placebo  Ibuprofen  Placebo  Ibuprofen  Placebo  Ibuprofen
These findings underscore the importance of considering both developmental stages and potential genetic influences when
assessing physical fitness levels in youth populations. Such insights could inform targeted interventions aimed at enhancing
physical activity and overall health in children and adolescents.
